sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
The patient reports a sore on his foot that is not healing.
History of Present Illness
The patient describes a wound on his right foot that began approximately two to three weeks ago and has not improved. He reports that he purchased a new pair of Hoka shoes to travel with and initially did not feel they were too tight, but the wound has worsened. He previously saw a podiatrist for a similar issue two years ago. He completed a course of antibiotics prescribed by his previous physician, but the wound remains. He notes that his sock sticks to the wound even when a bandage is applied. The patient's last HbA1c was slightly over eight. He reports experiencing occasional instability with the Hoka shoes.
Findings
Physical examination reveals a one by two inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, approximately proximal to the fifth to fifth metatarsal head (5th MTPA). The wound is covered with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue. There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, and no evidence of fluid collection or necrosis. There is no odor or bony exposure. Bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses are palpable. Doppler signs are present. The patient reports no pain with palpation.
Assessment

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
The patient presents with a chronic right foot ulcer, likely secondary to diabetic neuropathy and potential footwear-related pressure. No evidence of acute infection is present.
Plan
I have ordered an ankle brachial index (ABI) to assess blood flow to the foot and determine the likelihood of wound healing. I will perform a debridement of the wound in the office today. I am prescribing a collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, covered with a dry sterile dressing, and will continue this until we see the wound shrinking and a nice pink tissue is present. I am also ordering a surgical shoe to provide pressure relief. I will schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to reassess the wound and consider referral to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates suboptimal blood supply. I will also arrange a diabetic consult to discuss strategies for improving glycemic control and potentially modifying his diet to support wound healing.
